speaker: interviewer
text: Today you want to tell us about something about a farm. Could you please explain this incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning before the ghetto was closed, I'd been--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not in-- yes, all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1940, I think. I had been asked by one of our tenants in our building, Muranowska 17, if I want to come into a place, a farm not far from Warsaw, about 10, 15 kilometers from Warsaw. 20 kilometers. That was a farm-- Agrille, a Polish farm which was taken over by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does Agrille--

speaker: interviewee
text: For 500 zlotys, I can join the farm, and I can have work there and plenty of food. And I can maybe even do something to buy some food from that assessment and bring it to the ghetto. Or bring him something, bring to the ghetto. But I got to answering the questions because they're willing to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Interview you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Interview me. And I got to definitely not to say the [INAUDIBLE] on this person, but I'm a thief. And otherwise, he won't take me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the direction the people-- that your tenants said.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took me in there because he doesn't want any honest people. And the reason you'll hear when they talk to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you said the farm had a name. Would you repeat the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Agrille.

speaker: interviewer
text: Agrille.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Polish farm. And I said, all right. Let's go there. We'll give you the 500 zloty, but don't give them until I am sure that I am in it. He said, don't worry. He believes me because I'm a thief. You wasn't a thief, little boy. You're an honest boy. And he said, don't worry. I-- the 500 is promised when you are accepted. For sure, I won't give him. But I got to have the money in the pocket. Because if you are accepted and you say yes-- don't worry, you are in it. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: You hand it over.

speaker: interviewee
text: I give him the 500 zloty. So my father said-- wasn't too happy with that. Called me a thief. And so he said, all right. Go there. For food-- we do anything to get the food there. Especially from a farm with a person like that. I went there and he start to interview me, and he asked me questions. And he said, I don't know about you, that you are a thief, that you are good. That's what I need here, because, you see, when they come to pick up the food-- the Germans--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the German forces?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans come up to pick up the food. The-- for example, the Air Force--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sending the trucks and so you go down to the weight-- where the weight is underneath-- and you pull down to make-- two or four boys, five boys-- to make it a little bit heavier. And then when it's gone out, you pull up to make it heavier this way, and lower this way, until we'll have the, the weight that we'll give him. A hundred, 200 kilogram less, and then we can sell the potatoes or things like that. We'll do it. And nothing to worry, he said. I know who you are. That's good. But remember, if you get sometimes milk from here, that's all right. You pinch a little bit of milk. But remember to stay away from the cream. Because if I catch you touching the cream-- because there's so little cream-- then I will kill you on the spot. That was the, the-- his advice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Direction.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he used to come up to us when we went to sleep there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the loft.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we didn't go home. Every three, four days he'd let us go home. Now, when he come up, he used to tell us, what is happening to him? He's an SS man. He was let out of the prison in 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of what prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: The prison in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany. In German prison because he was a thief, a well-known thief. Yes. And he was let out and he joined the SS. That because he was let out, he joined the SS and the army. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't feel afraid of him, being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Being Jewish, you didn't feel afraid of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: If I was afraid of him?

speaker: interviewer
text: You personally. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you deal with a thief, and he said who he is.

speaker: interviewer
text: A code.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his, his, his interest was only how to get from the ghetto-- not ghetto, but from the Jews-- nice materials which he paid for it. He paid for us for the materials in food or in, in, in material like, like shovels, which we did need for the ghetto. Things which we did need--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tools. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --he supplied us. We did-- and he used to drive us home. Led me to the part, as close as he can to get us away in a cart with a horse. He used to do everything to help us because he was a thief. He wasn't-- he was an SS man only on the sign. But the rest of it, he was a normal thief which did want to make money. He was very useful for one thing. He told us about the movement of the German army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a very nice way. Not to put in the Germans in a bad thing. He said, look, they-- we did have bombed such-and-such city in Germany. We did have bombed this city. They are close already to my city. In a way, not to tell us in a normal way. But he used to say what is happening. He got a loose mouth because he was a thief. He wasn't interested in the SS--

speaker: interviewer
text: But what was the purpose-- yeah. What was the purpose of that to you, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: The purpose for us--

speaker: interviewer
text: His information. His information.

speaker: interviewee
text: To give it the ghetto to say that this is bombed already. This is-- that's good. That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: News. Filtering news.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lift us. Lift our morale. With the things that we know at least what is a little bit happening outside our ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. I see. So how long were you there? How long did you stay on that farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We'd been staying there a very short time, unfortunately. After five weeks. They started to suspect something is there. They start to suspect--

speaker: interviewer
text: Something such as what?

speaker: interviewee
text: The thievery from the gunner. He used to do anything he could-- anything-- to pinch everything. He said to us, look you've got chickens here. You are not allowed to cook chickens here. Not allowed. But if you see a crooked one with a leg or something, that has to be killed. Because I don't want any sick chicken there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he found himself that there was a lot of crooked there with me being there. Because we'd been eating chickens all day and every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was a place where we did dealt with the thief, SS man thief. And that was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you supplied the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we did have the ghetto with food.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It doesn't matter us or anybody, but we brought to the ghetto food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: 20-- Nearly 20 people are bringing food everyday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was--

speaker: interviewee
text: One on the first day, one on the second day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was a good supply.

speaker: interviewee
text: A good supply.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good. David, the day-to-day living of the ghetto-- and there were an enormous number of children there. How-- how did the day-to-day living of the ghetto work? And did those children have schooling? Did they roam wildly? How did it work?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, they did have a little bit of schooling organized by the teachers. Yes. They had been looked after.

speaker: interviewer
text: By what teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had been looked-- by the Jewish teachers, which came to the ghetto. They had been looked after in the beginning. But then more and more you found in the street dead children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would that be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the parents had been taken away from them and nobody to look after them. [INAUDIBLE], Mr. Czerniakow, I got not a good word for him. I don't want to say in particular. But the no good word is for me about Czerniakow. That Czerniakow could give the children-- call them in to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Central control.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the [NON-ENGLISH]. What you call [NON-ENGLISH]? The-- you said it before. What is it called?

speaker: interviewer
text: Community hall?

speaker: interviewee
text: The community hall and give the children chocolate. Immediately, they got diarrhea after that, and they been sick. Because they didn't eat anything but chocolate. He did give them chocolate. To help the kids, he gave them chocolate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they fell ill again.

speaker: interviewer
text: But--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the most important thing to me, what it did to us, was that to commit suicide without advising and helping the underground. Because he could help the underground very much. By helping the underground, if he would have said what is happening and why he commit suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, why he committed suicide?

speaker: interviewee
text: He committed suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I--

speaker: interviewee
text: If he would have said to us, that they force me now to send people to the-- and that's a gas chamber, remember-- such and such things happening. If he would have advised us at the time when he committed suicide, the story would be not much better but a little bit better. We would know how to fight with the older people. Because they would listen to him that they got killed. Because when they hear it from us, from the young one from the organization, you are killed there. That's at Treblinka. That's at such a camp, such a camp. You're going to be killed. They didn't believe us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. David, let me ask you--

speaker: interviewee
text: To him, they would--

speaker: interviewer
text: They would have believed him.

speaker: interviewee
text: They would believe him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I understand. Let me ask you. These children that were roaming without parents. There was some schooling. So why couldn't they had been taken up and cared for instead of-- as schoolchildren in a form of an orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the old ones couldn't help themselves. So with strange children, they wouldn't help them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the Judenrat, which was a committee of Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat was-- the Judenrat was rotten from the ground to the-- to the head.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these children wandered aimlessly and died?

speaker: interviewee
text: And died. Definitely. Mostly children you see in the street dying. And children taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: You refer to the fact that there's-- you were arming--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was a few organizations which helped. Like Korczak-- and yes, yes they did. But not all the children. They couldn't. They couldn't manage it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You referred to the fact that you were preparing for the action that you knew was necessary. You, ZZW, and ZOB were preparing for the actions that you knew were necessary. Were you given advance notice that an action was going to be taken against the ghetto? Where did this feeling that you were going to take a stand come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: When? At the end, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: No--

speaker: interviewee
text: During the uprising? Or you mean during the whole time?

speaker: interviewer
text: During the whole time and the fact that they took out in two large amounts, they took so many Jews out of the ghetto, leaving such small numbers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we, we couldn't do much till the practically end when we knew that the rest of the Jews are going to be killed. We had been prepared and we do-- did the job on the inside. That means cleaning up ourselves inside our house. And then thinking of further. And that's-- came along when they suddenly said, all right. The Jews are going to [? Miller ?] Street where they go to [? kettle, ?] what I called the [? kettle. ?] And I start to select. And without any knowledge, without anything, we are broken, too. Because we don't know where we're going.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you--

speaker: interviewee
text: If we stay inside the ghetto, or outside the ghetto, or inside the [? kettle, ?] or outside. We are broken up, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: David, this is not clear. You said you are cleaning up yourselves. Please explain that so it can be understood.

speaker: interviewee
text: The cleaning up of ourselves is to get rid of the people which are working with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were-- your ZZW was infiltrated? Or were these Jewish people that were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Jewish people outside the organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were informers.

speaker: interviewee
text: The police, the Jewish police, informers, Gestapo people. I got even a photo of a man which did death penalty of a Jewish boy. And I called him here in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know very well his name. [? Hasklin ?] [? Yuda. ?] And that man--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was an informer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inf-- he was working with the Gestapo in a way that he used to take Jews to the cemetery and saying to give him the money what they got or the goods. And if they said, they haven't got it, he asked the Germans to shoot him. Yes. He was here. He was caught by me. He was given to the police.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was here. He was caught by you.

speaker: interviewee
text: By me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here-- Australia? Here-- Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Australia. In Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Came here to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the police arrested him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did give him to the hands of the police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The police asked me in [? Russelstead ?] to bring a document of five people signed. Till 8 o'clock in the morning because I brought him in at 12 o'clock in the night. And when I came in the next day at 8 or 9 o'clock in the morning, and brought them in the papers, they said they let him out. When I asked him, why, [INAUDIBLE] finish up with it. When I asked them why they let him out, from here, he said that we checked that with Germany. Because when here is night, there is day. We checked with the Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's Nilemburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nuremberg. There's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nuremberg. We checked it. His name is not there. Definitely a little stinker. How would he be there? So we let him out. I was chasing him--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a moment. So you, you still presented the police with the signed document of names? Yes. For sure. For sure. And didn't they say they were going to arrest him again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. Nothing. Nothing. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They let him because he's not in the Nuremberg book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Files.

speaker: interviewee
text: Files. Now what we did, he went to Adelaide.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said, what we did. Who is we?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends, and myself here, and elsewhere. But they did want to put him where he should be put.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the police or to court. Now he went to Adelaide. I chased him in Adelaide. I advised some of my friends, Christian friends. And luckily, luckily, that's what you call it? That something happened which I am not a believer but God helped us at the time. He pinched some cars or something. He was in prison and then he died. Apparently, apparently-- I am not sure of it-- in prison. Then I got the federal police--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. He died apparently imprisoned.

speaker: interviewee
text: In prison or outside the prison. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of his own accord or--

speaker: interviewee
text: He died.

speaker: interviewer
text: He died. Fine, fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know of which accord. He died. And we-- the federal police came.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this we're talking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When are we talking? What year are we talking about, this gentleman?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're talking about 196something.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Fine. So this is-- just a moment. This is the ev-- now, what else in the everyday life of the ghetto was significant that the world needs to know that's part of your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: That the world needs to know?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's part of your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of my story? Part of my story which I should--

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like you to tell us the story of your niece.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please, of Naomi, your niece.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, my niece. That's a young girl of about six which I took out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- she was your niece from which side?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother. My wife's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife's sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's sister's daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Six years old and we did have to transport her to the Christian side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year are we talking, David?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're talking 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you have to transport her to the Christian side?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we didn't want to live in the ghetto. Because on the Christian side, they did have a nanny. Nanny-- nanny-- which we used to call her when she was a little child. And she approved that she will take her as her own child on the Christian side which would be safe for the kid. For the child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would be be safe for her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you manage to get her our of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took her. I put a sling over me and put a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A sling.

speaker: interviewee
text: A sling. And she was sitting in this thing. She was very, very, very thin, very small on her-- for her age. She's still alive in Mexico. And I put on a very big coat. But I still did look like a man pregnant. And we paid the, the boys there--

speaker: interviewer
text: The guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guards. We paid them to smuggle in. And all the 40 boys did get a bread each. All-- what 40 boys?

speaker: interviewer
text: All the boys who marched out with her. Because we marched out in a group of about 50 boys to clean bricks. To clean bricks. So this was a work party. This was a work party going out.

words.text: So this was a work party.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And those boys did help us in a way. Because we did arranged to go out. And about three, three, three flats further and one of-- inside of a-- of a-- of a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Corridor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not corridor. But inside the entrance, the woman will wait for the child to be delivered.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the previous nanny. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The nanny--

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember. That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know the name very well, but I wouldn't like to mention the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could I just interrupt and ask you-- each one of these 50 in the work party knew that you were smuggling a child--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Every one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they had been paid for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did this transfer occur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, when I went there, when we went out, we start by providing a little incident where one boy start to run towards the place. And we ran after him screaming, stop, stop, stop, to the boy. And we go next to the entrance. We're blocking the entrance completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: With body-- with body power.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can't go in, and you can't go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was with body power you blocked the entrance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with our-- 50 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the entrance is about three foot, four foot wide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the entrance is about 15. But this is closed. Only three, four foot to walk in and walk out of the place. That was-- that's how that was built there in Poland before the war-- or in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we blocked completely. I took out the child. Give it to the lady. She went to the-- to a-- one of the passengers there. And she start to ask people for a name which is not existent but with the child in the hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved away. And about 5, 10 minutes later, by her spending the time of asking for someone who does not exist there, she walked up with the child. So there wasn't anything suspicious that you're walking out with the child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sounds an incredible action. But didn't that lady need papers for the child? Weren't people--

speaker: interviewee
text: She did have everything. Her brother was a policeman. A Christian policeman.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the correct papers were available.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything she did have for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Naomi lived safely with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She lived safely with her until after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister-in-law and brother-in-law were saved?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They had been killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were killed. Were they taken to camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most probably-- um, no. Her father was killed at the Miller Street when was the cleaning. Her mother was killed by the Poles, which she was there with them. Because they did want to find out from [INAUDIBLE] where we live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And where her child lives. And she didn't want to do give away the, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We know the name of the Pole. We advised it after the War to the Polish government, to a Jewish fellow, which was a cousin of my wife. Advised them. And he was a, a, a, a judge in the new government. And he said to us when we went to him with the name, everything is excellent. He said, we didn't came here to fight now. We came here to build a socialist--

speaker: interviewer
text: State.

speaker: interviewee
text: System.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Naomi as she was left an orphan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Naomi was living with us after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: With Luba and yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I smuggled her up to Germany because I was working in the Bricha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you have to smuggle her after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, we have to smuggle her to Germany from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: But why?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't a free way to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Poland. Yes. Communists. Sorry. OK. So you had to again smuggle her out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Smuggle her out to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Under false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Under--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We smuggled the border by the help of the-- of the Haganah, of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Israeli underground. The Haganah. They were working in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: They we're working in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were working-- not Haganah, but the other I said to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bricha.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bricha. Yes. It was working everywhere. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd been working on the border of Czechoslovakia and Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you smuggle the nanny with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We got her back from the nanny in a very hard way. That was very hard to get her back. But we got her back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was she having had that--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was paid for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many-- no, no, no. How many years was Naomi with the nanny?

speaker: interviewee
text: '42, '43, '44. About three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was she traumatized when she came back to you and found out--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was very--

speaker: interviewer
text: And found out that her parents were dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: --very upset with everything that was happening. Very upset. Yes. She thought that this is her mother and she wants back to Nanna. She doesn't want to come back to us. She knew who we are. She knew well. She remembered even me taking her from her mother. She did remember her mother. She did remember. But she wants with Nanna-- not with us. But slowly, slowly, slowly she came with us. She went to Germany with us. And then in Germany, a plane, a military plane flew in to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the plane was her uncle, the brother of her father, from Mexico. How he got on a military plane, we still to today don't know about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year are we talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1946 or '47. '46. He came into Germany. And he told us, look, you've got no way at the moment how to live in the new-- we'll help you. We'll take you maybe to Mexico, if you want to. But give us the child. Because there she'll be brought up the right way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now--

speaker: interviewee
text: He did have papers already for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was about 9 or 10 at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a 9-year-old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did she feel comfortable to go? Or did anybody ask her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she felt comfortable because she knew that-- she already grow up to know what that mean. Her uncle, is-- which is a brother of her father. She knew. He was looking like-- he was very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Similar?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was-- he was like her father in appearance and the look. So-- a very well known family in Mexico and a very well known family in Europe altogether. The [? Nitskins. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Nitskins. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have children of their own?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those people-- yes, they did. And they have sister, and children-- and they met her up there. She married there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a second. She fitted in very comfortably?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. From the letters we did have-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. So you're in touch with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We are in touch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brilliant.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was here. We brought her here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brilliant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. As a matter of fact, on my desk is a-- is a letter from her what I got from her. Because I got a write-up in the book about her happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: And David--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she wrote to me that she received it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family disappeared completed. Was killed. Killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were killed. Inside the ghetto or were they taken on a transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: 99% Inside. And one brother and a sister went to the Hotel Polski. I don't know if you had heard about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened at the Hotel Polski?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had been killed in one of the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they killed? David, I may have heard--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they registered themselves-- they registered themselves for America instead of Israel. The Israelis-- the people which registered in Hotel Polski for Israel, most of them are alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how-- who killed them then?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: That-- could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hotel Polski, after the-- you know, after-- during still the resident in the ghetto-- or-- or a little bit--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year are we talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm talking '43. The Germans made a new trick with Jewish people together. With Jewish Gestapo people-- working with the Gestapo. They made the Hotel Polski. You never heard of it?

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like your family to know about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they made the Hotel Polski hotel where the invite all the Jews to come freely to the hotel and they'd be exchanged for Germans overseas. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So a lot of Jews being tired of being on the Christian side, not having money, or people with a lot of money, thought themselves that's a good thing. We advised them, the underground, as much as we could, to tell them that's not so. That that's a new trick. ZOB advised them that's a new trick. But to no avail. They did it and they went there. My brother and my sister went there. And wrote me a letter to my place where I'd been hiding on the Christian side by that time to come to-- to come there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Hotel Polski situated?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Warsaw. On the Aryan side?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the Aryan side. And nobody was even touched in the street-- a Jew-- if you said, I am going to Hotel Polski. So Gestapo couldn't stop him. He is going to Hotel Polski.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they went to Hotel Polski, did they have to have money or were they just accepted?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They did have-- the ones who did have money did give to the Gestapo boys, to the Jewish boys which had been working in the Gestapo. And other ones, other boys which didn't have the money, they did want to show that they are good, that they are not working with the Gestapo. So they took him in a cover. That you, see without money we took in boys, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're saying the Jewish boys that worked with the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's, that's correct. That's correct. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many boys such as this were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was there about four or five of them which were working for the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said the people that came to Hotel Polski registered to go where? To Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Israel, to America, to South America. With false passport--

speaker: interviewer
text: By choice?

speaker: interviewee
text: With good passports. That I got invitation there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who issued them the passports?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Gestapo boys in the-- at the-- at the Hotel Polski.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you say--

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a book about Hotel Polski.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know. Who-- you say that those that were registered to go to Israel-- nobody was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them. Very few. The rest lived through. Because they did want to do something. They told that they'll get out. The, the, the very dangerous place for the Germans was Israel. Not Israel but Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from there, they did want to get them out as soon as they can, the Germans. So most probably that was the way that I'd been thinking. That's my opinion. That's not the opinion maybe of other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was an action in Hotel Polski. Is that where your family was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean an action?

speaker: interviewer
text: Wasn't there a bombing or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: A bombing?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? All right. My misunderstanding. OK. Now when did you leave the ghetto, you and Luba? When did you leave the ghetto? And did you leave part-time or permanent?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- no. I fixed a place for Luba in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: You fixed a place where for Luba?

speaker: interviewee
text: The place on the Aryan--

speaker: interviewer
text: Aryan side.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Aryan side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Luba, your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was she in false papers or Jewish papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: False papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the false papers gave her an identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Given to me by a man which lives here in Australia which got the, the, the-- from Israel the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Righteous Gentile.

speaker: interviewee
text: Righteous Gentile. Mr. Jan Kostanski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jan Kostanski.

speaker: interviewee
text: He lives here.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Melbourne?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: These papers gave Luba the freedom to walk freely in the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not necessarily because that was false papers, apparently. And she was blonde. Completely blonde. Using perhidrol to cover-- to, to, to wash her hair to get the blonde.

speaker: interviewer
text: Perhidrol.

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhidrol is a chemical which makes--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bleaches.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you from black. Bleaches very light. Bleaching the hair to make it blonde. But was a problem because they're growing still from the bottom up. And we did have a lot of work to keep it up. She is-- she was very thin and very small. Nobody thought-- she got only one accident where she give her passport to a German because they were controlling something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her identity papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And she ripped the passport and she start to run. He didn't want to shoot her, most probably, but to catch her. And he shot her in her leg very lightly-- a cut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did this fear suddenly overcome her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she feared that he will see that this is a false passport. She didn't want to stay there. She-- which my wife was, I think, very clever. Very good. If the Germans said left, she went right. If the Germans said right, she went left. If the German went forward, she went backward. If the German said backward, she went forward. Always opposite what the Germans said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was this instinct in her?

speaker: interviewee
text: In her was the instinct as a Jewess with a passport, with a false passport in the hands of the German. And so many people around there which is checking. She got the chance of ripping it out of his hand-- because he's lightly holding it-- and starting to run. He won't leave the rest of the people to run away completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she--

speaker: interviewee
text: So he let her run. And he shot. And he just slightly--

speaker: interviewer
text: Grazed her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her leg, yes. And she run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she, she melted into the crowd.

speaker: interviewee
text: She melted in there to the crowd.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Luba work or was she at home more? How did she occupy her time?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a, a maid at-- with the place where we paid money to be there. She occupied by reading. But we'd been going out, too. And we'd been walking around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I know it was often said that the Jew-- Jewish accent of Polish was different. When Luba spoke Polish, was her accent distinguishable from the general Pole?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, the problem wasn't as far as Polish to speak proper Polish. We speak too proper Polish, and we had been already told, start to speak like a peasant. Or like a Warsaw man. Not like an intelligent woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give us a word or two how you spoke and how a Polish person would speak? Say the same word.

speaker: interviewee
text: You will hear very often, [POLISH], [POLISH] No. [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Well which way was correct? [POLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [POLISH]. .

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: The one which-- we had been speaking too good Polish. Another thing is remember one thing. They didn't know only with the language. They did know something else. Your behavior was worse than the language. Don't wash your hands too quick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
